nativeRange.habitat: Tropical and subtropical Indo-West Pacific reef crests, reef flats, intertidal pools, and shallow subtidal rubble, where the star grazes biofilm and detritus across live rock and sediment.
nativeRange.biotope: Indo-West Pacific coral reef and shallow rubble

decorPreferences: mature live rock with abundant biofilm and microalgae
fine sand or coral rubble substrate

diet.notes: In the wild, the Luzon Sea Star feeds primarily on the bacterial and algal films (biofilm) that coat sediment and live rock surfaces, supplementing this with detritus and small encrusting organisms. In captivity, it has been reported to accept flake fragments and finely chopped meaty foods such as mysis and brine shrimp, and it will graze actively across mature live rock. A well-established system with continuous biofilm growth is important; starvation has been reported in sparsely developed tanks where food sources are insufficient.

avoidWith: large angelfishes that may nip starfish
small clams and tridacnids (occasional reports of damage)

summary: The Luzon Sea Star (Echinaster luzonicus) is a small Indo-Pacific reef star, typically six-armed and often lopsided in appearance as a result of its habit of shedding arms to reproduce. Generally considered one of the more aquarium-tolerant sea stars, it still requires a mature, biofilm-rich reef to thrive.
careNotes: The Luzon Sea Star is widely sold under multiple names — Orange Sea Star, Asian Red Sea Star, and Comet Sea Star are common alternatives — and is recognizable by its typically six (rather than five) arms and frequently lopsided appearance. The asymmetry is a consequence of its main reproductive strategy: the star routinely sheds an arm (a process called autotomy) that then regrows into a complete new individual, producing the classic single-arm-with-bud 'comet' form that is sometimes sold separately. Color varies from orange-red to deep brown, and individuals have been reported to shift between morphs. The species is generally considered one of the more aquarium-tolerant Echinaster sea stars and is often described as beginner-friendly by retailers, but in practice a mature reef system is strongly recommended — typically at least six months of run time, with abundant live rock, microfauna, and established biofilm; hobbyists have reported specimens slowly starving in under-developed tanks. Like all echinoderms, the Luzon Sea Star is highly sensitive to sudden changes in salinity, pH, alkalinity, and oxygen levels, and a slow drip acclimation of at least two hours is generally advised when introducing it to a new aquarium. Copper-based medications are toxic to sea stars and must not be used in any system housing this species. Reef compatibility is generally good with corals, but the star has been reported to graze on prized sponges and may occasionally damage small clams, so keepers of show sponges or tridacnids (giant clams and their relatives) should monitor the star's behavior carefully. Lifespan in aquaria is poorly documented; although some accounts suggest decades are possible in the wild, realistic expectations in the hobby are several years in a well-maintained system.
breedingNotes: The Luzon Sea Star reproduces primarily by arm autotomy — the star sheds an arm, which then regenerates a new central disc and the remaining arms to become a complete individual. This process produces the characteristic 'comet' growth form, a single arm with a small bud at the base that is sometimes sold as a separate animal. Sexual reproduction has been documented in this species but is not how new individuals typically appear in aquariums. Fragments and comet-stage animals encountered in the trade are the result of this natural asexual process.

primary.name: ReefGuide.org
primary.notes: LiveAquaria's species page (the slice's designated primary) returned 404 at access time; primary role was swapped to ReefGuide.org, which carries the species ID, family, range, and size data.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Echinasteridae, Spinulosida), Indo-West Pacific distribution, asymmetry/six-arm biology, autotomy-based asexual reproduction, and diet (bacterial and algal films from sediment).

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked depth range (0-73 m), temperature range (24.2-28.9 °C), and minimum tank-size recommendation (>=300 L). Reeflex's adult-size figure (65 cm) is an outlier inconsistent with every other source and is treated as erroneous; the 13-15 cm range used here matches ReefGuide and reef-aquarium-store.com.

additional.name: Reef Aquarium Store
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~15 cm), peaceful temperament, omnivorous scavenger diet, and reef-safe status. Listed as beginner-friendly; this entry leans toward intermediate to reflect the mature-reef and feeding requirements emphasized by hobbyist reports.

additional.name: Build Your Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked reef-safe status, detritivorous/omnivorous grazing diet, and the need for a mature reef with adequate biofilm. Recommends medium-to-large reef tanks.

additional.name: Reef Central forum (community)
additional.notes: Hobbyist reports flag slow starvation as the main long-term failure mode in tanks that lack established biofilm and detritus; informed the careNotes guidance on mature-reef setups.
